title: Overriding and Specialization
summary: Override a parent method when a subclass needs a different result, while still reusing shared setup from the base class.

sketchBlocks.title: Why subclasses override methods
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A subclass inherits behavior from a base class, but sometimes one method needs to work differently for that specific subtype. That is when you **override** the parent method: you keep the same method name, but write a new version in the subclass.

Imagine a catalog app with a general `Item` and a special `Book`. Most items might show a plain label, but books may need the author in the label too.

```python
class Item:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def display_name(self):
        return self.name

class Book(Item):
    def __init__(self, name, author):
        super().__init__(name)
        self.author = author

    def display_name(self):
        return f"{self.name} by {self.author}"
```

Step by step:

- `Item.display_name()` returns the basic item name.
- `Book` inherits from `Item`, so it already gets `name` handling.
- `super().__init__(name)` reuses the parent setup instead of rewriting it.
- `Book.display_name()` overrides the parent version because books need a more specific result.

The important idea is that the subclass keeps the **same method contract** but provides a more specialized result.

sketchBlocks.title: Reusing parent initialization with super()
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When a subclass adds new attributes, it often still needs the base class to set up shared attributes first. That is why `super()` is useful.

```python
class Item:
    def __init__(self, name, price):
        self.name = name
        self.price = price

class DigitalItem(Item):
    def __init__(self, name, price, file_size_mb):
        super().__init__(name, price)
        self.file_size_mb = file_size_mb
```

Line by line:

- `Item.__init__` stores the shared data: `name` and `price`.
- `DigitalItem` needs those shared attributes too.
- `super().__init__(name, price)` calls the parent constructor.
- After that, `DigitalItem` adds its own attribute: `file_size_mb`.

Without `super()`, you would repeat the parent setup manually. Reusing the parent constructor keeps the code shorter and safer, especially if the base class changes later.

sketchBlocks.title: Specialized behavior still supports polymorphism
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Overriding is especially useful when a service works with a mixed list of related objects. Each object can respond to the same method name in its own way.

```python
class Item:
    def shipping_note(self):
        return "Standard shipping"

class FragileItem(Item):
    def shipping_note(self):
        return "Handle with care"

items = [Item(), FragileItem()]
for item in items:
    print(item.shipping_note())
```

What happens here:

- Both classes provide a `shipping_note()` method.
- The loop does not need separate logic for each subtype.
- A plain `Item` returns `"Standard shipping"`.
- A `FragileItem` overrides that method and returns `"Handle with care"`.

This is the start of **polymorphism**: one loop, one shared method name, different specialized results depending on the object type.

quizDraft.title: Override a label method for books
quizDraft.prompt: You are building a small catalog app. In `models/item.py`, the base `Item` class already returns the item name from `display_name()`. In `models/book.py`, complete the `Book` subclass so it reuses the parent constructor with `super()` and overrides `display_name()` to return `"<name> by <author>"`. Keep the shared method name the same so the catalog can call it consistently.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Open the workspace files and complete the Book class.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Open the workspace files and complete the Book class.

semanticChecks.message: Define a Book class.
Book should be constructible with name and author.
Book instances should store both name and author.
Book.display_name() should return the specialized label.

quizDraft.hint: The subclass should keep `display_name()` but return a more specific string than the base class.

help.concept: Overriding means a subclass defines a method with the same name as the parent method so that calls on subclass objects use the specialized version.
help.hint_1: Use `super().__init__(...)` to set the inherited `name` attribute before storing `author`.
help.hint_2: The overridden method should combine the inherited `name` with the subclass's `author` in one formatted string.

quizDraft.title: Reuse parent initialization in a digital item
quizDraft.prompt: In this workspace, `Item` already stores `name` and `price`. Finish `DigitalItem` in `models/digital_item.py` so its constructor uses `super()` to reuse the parent initialization, then stores `file_size_mb`. Also override `summary()` so it returns `"<name> ($<price>) - <file_size_mb>MB download"` using the stored values.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Complete the DigitalItem class in the workspace files.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Complete the DigitalItem class in the workspace files.

semanticChecks.message: Define a DigitalItem class.
DigitalItem should accept name, price, and file_size_mb.
DigitalItem instances should store inherited and subclass attributes.
DigitalItem.summary() should return the specialized summary.

quizDraft.hint: Let the parent constructor handle the shared fields, then add the subclass field.

help.concept: A subclass constructor often calls `super().__init__` to reuse the base class setup before adding subclass-specific attributes and behavior.
help.hint_1: Pass `name` and `price` into `super().__init__(...)` exactly once in the subclass constructor.
help.hint_2: After the parent setup, store `file_size_mb`, then build the summary string from all three attributes.

quizDraft.title: Use one service loop with mixed item types
quizDraft.prompt: A shipping service should work with mixed item types through one shared method name. Complete `FragileItem` so it overrides `shipping_note()` to return `"Handle with care"`. Then complete `services/shipping.py` so `collect_shipping_notes(items)` loops through the list and returns a new list of each object's `shipping_note()` result in order.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Complete the subclass and service function in the workspace files.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Complete the subclass and service function in the workspace files.

semanticChecks.message: Define a FragileItem class.
FragileItem.shipping_note() should override the base behavior.

quizDraft.hint: Each object in the list should respond to the same method name, even if the returned text differs.

help.concept: Polymorphism lets one loop call the same method on different subclasses, while overriding lets each subclass provide its own specialized result.
help.hint_1: Override `shipping_note()` in `FragileItem`, but leave the base class method available for regular items.
help.hint_2: In the service function, build a list by calling `item.shipping_note()` for every object in `items`.

quizDraft.title: Identify the overridden method
quizDraft.prompt: A base class `Item` has a method named `display_name()`. A subclass `Book` also defines `display_name()` with the same parameter list. What is this called?
quizDraft.hint: The subclass keeps the same method name but changes the behavior.

help.concept: Overriding happens when a subclass provides its own implementation of a method already defined in the base class.
help.hint_1: Look for the term that describes replacing inherited behavior in a subclass.
help.hint_2: It is not creating a brand-new unrelated method; it is redefining an inherited one with the same name.

quizDraft.title: Why call super() in a subclass constructor
quizDraft.prompt: Why would a subclass constructor call `super().__init__(name, price)` in a catalog app?
options: To reuse the parent setup for shared attributes
To turn the subclass into a base class
To prevent the subclass from having new attributes
To make every method return the same value
quizDraft.hint: Think about which class already knows how to store the shared attributes.

help.concept: `super()` lets a subclass reuse the base class constructor so shared setup is not duplicated.
help.hint_1: The base class already handles common fields like `name` and `price`.
help.hint_2: The subclass can let the parent set shared attributes first, then add its own extra attributes.

quizDraft.title: Keep the shared method name
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the subclass method name so the service can call the same method on every item type.
quizDraft.hint: The subclass should use the same method name as the base class method it replaces.

help.concept: For overriding to work, the subclass method must use the same name as the base class method so shared code can call it consistently.
help.hint_1: The base class method in this example is the one that returns the label shown in the catalog.
help.hint_2: Choose the method name that matches the parent method exactly, not a new custom name.

quizDraft.title: Choose the parent setup call
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing expression that reuses the parent constructor inside a subclass `__init__`.
quizDraft.hint: You want the parent class to handle the shared attributes first.

help.concept: Inside a subclass constructor, `super().__init__(...)` calls the base class constructor to initialize inherited state.
help.hint_1: The missing code should call the parent constructor, not assign directly to `self`.
help.hint_2: Choose the expression that starts with `super()` and passes the shared arguments into `__init__`.

quizDraft.title: Choose the true statements about overriding
quizDraft.prompt: Which statements are true about overriding in this topic? Choose all that apply.
options: A subclass can keep the same method name but return a more specific result.
Overriding helps a service work with mixed object types through one shared method call.
A subclass must delete the parent method before it can specialize behavior.
Overriding means every subclass method must return exactly the same text as the base class.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what stays the same and what changes when a subclass specializes behavior.

help.concept: Overriding keeps a shared method contract while allowing subclasses to return different results and still work in shared loops or services.
help.hint_1: Look for statements about same method names, specialized behavior, and mixed-object services.
help.hint_2: Avoid choices that claim every subclass must behave identically or that overriding removes the need for inheritance.

quizDraft.title: Order the subclass setup steps
quizDraft.prompt: Put these steps in the best order for building a subclass that specializes a base class method in the catalog app.
tokens: Declare the subclass with the base class in parentheses
Define the overridden method with the same name
correctOrder: Declare the subclass with the base class in parentheses
Define the overridden method with the same name
quizDraft.hint: Start by inheriting from the base class, then reuse shared setup before adding specialized behavior.

help.concept: A clean subclass usually starts by inheriting from the base class, reusing parent initialization, adding subclass state, and then overriding the needed method.
help.hint_1: The parent constructor call belongs before storing subclass-only attributes.
help.hint_2: The overridden method comes after the constructor work because it uses the stored attributes.

quizDraft.title: Specialize labels for two catalog subclasses
quizDraft.prompt: You are extending a media catalog. The base `CatalogItem` already stores `title`. Complete both subclasses so they reuse the parent constructor and override `display_name()`: `BookItem` should return `"<title> by <author>"`, and `MovieItem` should return `"<title> (<minutes> min)"`. The workspace already includes a small `main.py` that prints both labels.

semanticChecks.message: Define a BookItem class.
Define a MovieItem class.

semanticChecks.expected: Clean Code by Robert C. Martin
semanticChecks.message: BookItem.display_name() should return the book label.
MovieItem.display_name() should return the movie label.

quizDraft.hint: Both subclasses share the same method name, but each one formats the result differently.

help.concept: Different subclasses can override the same base method in different ways while still fitting the same base-class contract.
help.hint_1: Use `super().__init__(title)` in both subclass constructors before storing the extra field.
help.hint_2: Write one `display_name()` method in each subclass, each returning its own specialized string format.

quizDraft.title: Override a pricing method for sale items
quizDraft.prompt: A store app has a base `Item` class with `final_price()` that returns the regular price. Complete `SaleItem` so it reuses the parent constructor and overrides `final_price()` to apply a percentage discount. For example, a price of `50` with a discount of `20` should return `40.0`. Keep the method name `final_price()` so pricing services can call it on any item type.

semanticChecks.message: Define a SaleItem class.
SaleItem should store inherited and subclass attributes.
SaleItem.final_price() should apply the discount.
SaleItem.final_price() should work for another discount case too.

quizDraft.hint: The subclass should compute a reduced price from the inherited price and its own discount percentage.

help.concept: A subclass can override a calculation method so shared services still call the same method name while each subtype computes its own result.
help.hint_1: Store `discount_percent` in the subclass after calling `super().__init__(name, price)`.
help.hint_2: Compute the discount amount from `self.price * self.discount_percent / 100`, then subtract it from `self.price`.

quizDraft.title: Build a report from mixed specialized items
quizDraft.prompt: You now have a mixed catalog report. Complete the subclasses and the service so `services/reporting.py` defines `build_display_report(items)`, which returns a list of each object's `display_name()` result. The list may contain base `CatalogItem` objects, `BookItem` objects, and `MovieItem` objects. The service should not check types manually; it should rely on the shared method name.

semanticChecks.message: BookItem.display_name() should return the specialized book label.
MovieItem.display_name() should return the specialized movie label.
The workspace should support creating base catalog items for mixed lists.

quizDraft.hint: The service loop should trust that every object in the list supports `display_name()`.

help.concept: Polymorphic services work by calling the same method on mixed related objects, while overriding lets each subclass provide its own specialized output.
help.hint_1: Make sure each subclass overrides `display_name()` correctly before writing the service loop.
help.hint_2: In the service function, iterate through `items` and append `item.display_name()` for each object in order.

quizDraft.title: Add an audiobook subclass with its own summary
quizDraft.prompt: The catalog is gaining audiobooks. Complete `AudioBookItem` so it inherits from `BookItem`, reuses parent initialization for `title` and `author`, stores `narrator`, and overrides `display_name()` to return `"<title> by <author> (narrated by <narrator>)"`. This is a specialization of an already specialized class.

semanticChecks.message: Define an AudioBookItem class.
AudioBookItem should store inherited and new attributes.

semanticChecks.expected: Dune by Frank Herbert (narrated by Simon Vance)
semanticChecks.message: AudioBookItem.display_name() should return the audiobook label.

quizDraft.hint: This subclass should build on `BookItem`, not start over from the base item class.

help.concept: A subclass can inherit from another subclass, reuse its constructor with `super()`, and still override a method again for an even more specific result.
help.hint_1: `BookItem` already knows how to store `title` and `author`, so call its constructor from `AudioBookItem`.
help.hint_2: After storing `narrator`, override `display_name()` with a string that includes title, author, and narrator.

quizDraft.title: Specialize shipping cost for physical and digital products
quizDraft.prompt: Finish a small fulfillment model. The base `Product` class should keep `shipping_cost()` returning `5`. `DigitalProduct` should override it to return `0`, and `HeavyProduct` should override it to return `12`. Then complete `services/fulfillment.py` so `total_shipping(products)` returns the sum of `shipping_cost()` across a mixed list. The service should rely on the shared method name instead of separate type checks.

semanticChecks.message: Product.shipping_cost() should return the base shipping cost.
DigitalProduct.shipping_cost() should override the base cost.
HeavyProduct.shipping_cost() should override the base cost.

quizDraft.hint: Each subclass changes only the shipping calculation, while the service loop stays simple.

help.concept: Overriding lets subclasses specialize one calculation, and polymorphism lets a shared service sum results from mixed object types through one common method.
help.hint_1: Give each subclass its own `shipping_cost()` return value while keeping the same method name as the base class.
help.hint_2: In `total_shipping(products)`, loop through the list and add `product.shipping_cost()` for each object.
